title: Dermatopharmacokinetics: factors influencing drug clearance from the stratum corneum.

formulations.dose_amount: 500 µl over 2.5 cm diameter area, 30 min application
formulations.evidence_snippet: the total amount of ibuprofen (in µg/cm2) within the SC hardly changed at all during the 4 hours clearance period (Table II)

notes: Table II (referenced as 'Table II' in the paper text at page 12) reports total ibuprofen amount (µg/cm²) retained in the stratum corneum at six clearance timepoints (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 4 h post-removal of formulation). The ENDPOINT_TABLE_HINT 'Table 2' matches this table. The actual numeric values from Table II are NOT reproduced in the extracted page text; only the qualitative finding is stated ('hardly changed at all during the 4 hours clearance period'). Accordingly, endpoint_value is set to null for all six records. The formulation is a saturated ibuprofen solution (~10 mg/ml) in 75:25 (v/v) propylene glycol/water, applied for 30 min (500 µl, finite dose) to human ventral forearm skin in vivo. No receptor medium is applicable (in vivo DPK / tape-strip study). Six records are emitted — one per timepoint. Additional pages containing Table II numeric data should be retrieved to populate endpoint_value fields.

title: Two-Photon Polymerisation 3D Printing of Microneedle Array Templates with Versatile Designs: Application in the Development of Polymeric Drug Delivery Systems.

formulations.api_basis: initial drug content of lyophilised wafer-like reservoir
formulations.table_id: Table II (context: in vitro permeation narrative)
formulations.evidence_snippet: The D6 HFMN arrays led to a higher total amount of IBU Na delivery, reaching approximately 32 ± 8% of the initial drug content of the lyophilised wafer-like reservoir (100 mg).
formulations.notes: D6 design: 5×5 conical needles, 0.5 cm2 array area, 1.2 mm needle height, 500 μm base width, 1 mm tip-to-tip needle spacing. IBU Na quantified by RP-HPLC-UV (Table II: range 0.08–50 μg/mL, r2=1.0000). Endpoint value is approximate (32 ± 8%).

formulations.api_basis: initial drug content of lyophilised wafer-like reservoir
formulations.table_id: Table II (context: in vitro permeation narrative)
formulations.evidence_snippet: in the case of the D7 HFMN arrays, the drug amount measured in the acceptor [truncated — value not shown in provided pages]
formulations.notes: D7 design: 5×3 cross-shaped needles, 0.5 cm2 array area, 1.3 mm needle height, 150 μm lateral width, 1 mm tip-to-tip needle spacing. Endpoint value for D7 was truncated before it could be extracted. IBU Na quantified by RP-HPLC-UV (Table II: range 0.08–50 μg/mL, r2=1.0000).

notes: Table II (page 8) provides RP-HPLC-UV validation parameters for IBU Na (range 0.08–50 μg/mL, slope 90.92, y-intercept 3.44, r2=1.0000, LOD 0.07 μg/mL, LOQ 0.22 μg/mL in PBS pH 7.4) — this is an analytical validation table, not a formulation composition or endpoint table per se, but it confirms the analytical method for ibuprofen quantification. The HFMN composition (Gantrez S-97 / PEG 10,000 / Na2CO3) is described in the methods text (page 3), not in a dedicated composition table. Endpoint data for both D6 and D7 at 24 h come from narrative in page 9 (Fig. 9 referenced but not reproduced). The D7 endpoint value was truncated. The TABLE_TRUNCATED flag is set; additional endpoint data (e.g., cumulative permeation profiles) may be present in pages not supplied or in Fig. 9.

title: Design and development of a novel fused filament fabrication (FFF) 3D printed diffusion cell with UV imaging capabilities to characterise permeation in pharmaceutical formulations.
notes: The selected pages (5, 8, 9, 10, 11) contain no explicit composition tables or endpoint summary tables. The text describes a 5% w/w ibuprofen gel (commercially purchased from Vivomed) used as the model formulation, with phosphate buffer pH 7.2 as receptor medium, a 0.13 mm thick Silatos silicone membrane (Atos Medical, Sweden), diffusion area of 3.14 cm², ~1 mL dose volume, 12 h experiment at 37 °C, and 600 rpm stirring. No tabulated excipient compositions, endpoint values, cumulative permeation data, or flux values are present in the provided page text. Quantitative permeation results appear to reside in figures (Figure 6 area) and possibly later pages not included in the selection.

notes: The paper describes four formulations: (1) IBUGEL 5% w/w gel (contains IMS + PG), (2) IBULEVE Speed Relief 5% Spray (contains IMS, no PG), (3) Ibuprofen 5% w/w in IPA/PEG 300, (4) Ibuprofen 5% w/w in IPA/PG. Permeation studies were conducted in four membrane models: human skin (up to 48 h, finite dose), porcine skin (up to 48 h, finite dose), Skin PAMPA (up to 6 h; doses of 1, 3, and 30 µL studied; 1 µL confirmed as representative finite dose), and silicone membrane 250 µm (up to 6 h, finite dose). Table 1 in the paper is an excipient-function table, not a numeric composition or endpoint table. Quantitative per-formulation endpoint values are presented in Figures 1 and 2 (not extracted here as they are graphical). Only aggregate ranges are available in the text: human skin 48 h: 11–38 µg/cm²; porcine skin 48 h: 59–81 µg/cm²; PAMPA/silicone 6 h finite dose: 91–136 µg/cm². PAMPA 1 µL commercial formulations at 6 h: ~90–100 µg/cm² (64–69% applied dose). PAMPA 3 µL commercial formulations at 6 h: ~280 µg/cm² (60–70% applied dose). Precise per-formulation disaggregated endpoint values require the figures, which are not available in the selected pages.

title: Controlling Supramolecular Structures of Drugs by Light.

formulations.notes: Trans-isomer had a lag time of ~18 h and reached equilibrium after 72 h. No numerical flux or cumulative amount table provided; values described qualitatively in text/Figure 4C.

components.remark: Used as comparator; both P+-Ibuprofen ion-pairs penetrated significantly better than sodium salt

formulations.evidence_snippet: Both Ibuprofen ion-pairs penetrated significantly better than the sodium salt (Figure 4C)
formulations.notes: Comparator formulation only; no concentration or numerical endpoint provided in text.

notes: No formal composition or endpoint tables with numerical permeation/release data are present in the extracted pages. The article describes in vitro permeation experiments through Strat-M® synthetic skin membranes using 8 mM aqueous solutions of P+-Ibuprofen ion-pairs (cis vs. trans isomers) and an Ibuprofen sodium comparator, with results shown only in Figure 4C (a graph). Qualitative endpoint times (equilibrium at <12 h for cis, ~72 h for trans; lag time ~18 h for trans) are extracted from narrative text. No tabulated cumulative amount, flux, or Jss values are available. TABLE_TRUNCATED flag is set; supplementary data may contain additional quantitative tables. Recommended next action is manual review or retrieval of supplementary information.

notes: No formal composition or endpoint table was found in the provided pages. The paper describes ibuprofen (and lidocaine) loaded into porous polymeric microneedles at a 1:1 resin:drug mass ratio, with release measured in DPBS. The only quantitative endpoint explicitly stated in text is ~20% cumulative release at 200 min. A release profile figure (Fig. 4d) is referenced but its tabulated data is not available in the provided page content. Additional pages (especially the methods/results pages containing Fig. 4 data and any supplementary tables) would be needed for full endpoint extraction.

title: Microneedle pretreatment enhances the percutaneous permeation of hydrophilic compounds with high melting points.

formulations.evidence_snippet: Ibuprofen Control Jmax (μg/cm²/h) 167.78; 10-6 Papp (cm/s) 1.92; Recovery (%) 6.76
formulations.notes: Control = untreated skin. Jmax is the maximum flux from the linear part of the permeation curve. 80% saturated solution applied.

formulations.membrane_type: bovine split skin – microneedle pretreated (200 μm)
formulations.evidence_snippet: Ibuprofen 200 μm Jmax (μg/cm²/h) 368.69; 10-6 Papp (cm/s) 4.21; Recovery (%) 11.15
formulations.notes: Skin pretreated with 200 μm microneedle roller (192 titanium needles). 80% saturated solution applied.

formulations.membrane_type: bovine split skin – microneedle pretreated (300 μm)
formulations.evidence_snippet: Ibuprofen 300 μm Jmax (μg/cm²/h) 431.52; 10-6 Papp (cm/s) 5.27*; Recovery (%) 17.75; * = p<0.05
formulations.notes: Skin pretreated with 300 μm microneedle roller (192 titanium needles). 80% saturated solution applied. Statistically significant vs. control (p<0.05).

notes: Table 1 reports permeation parameters (Jmax, Papp, Recovery) for ibuprofen (and diclofenac, ketoprofen, paracetamol) across three skin pretreatment conditions: untreated control, 200 μm microneedle-pretreated, and 300 μm microneedle-pretreated bovine split skin. Only ibuprofen rows are extracted here per task scope. The formulation is an 80% saturated solution in PBS (vehicle not further specified in available text). Jmax (maximum flux) is used as the endpoint; Papp and Recovery are additional reported parameters but not separately extracted as endpoint records since Jmax is the primary permeation endpoint and the ENDPOINT_TABLE_HINT is Table 1 with flux focus. No endpoint time column is explicitly tabulated (Jmax is derived from the linear portion of the curve, not a single timepoint). Membrane thickness stated as 600 ± 50 μm (split skin). Receptor fluid: sonicated PBS (~12 mL), diffusion area ~1.77 cm², Franz-type diffusion cells. n = 5–6 per group.

title: Effects of Temperature and Humidity on the Skin Permeation of Hydrophilic and Hydrophobic Drugs.

formulations.notes: Environmental condition: T48RH95 (48°C, 95% RH, AH=71.7 g/m3, skin surface temp 40°C). Log P is permeability coefficient (log scale). Endpoint here reports Log P from Table III.
Environmental condition: T50RH55 (50°C, 55% RH, AH=45.6 g/m3, skin surface temp 40°C). Log P is permeability coefficient (log scale).
Environmental condition: T52RH30 (52°C, 30% RH, AH=27.3 g/m3, skin surface temp 40°C). Log P is permeability coefficient (log scale).
Environmental condition: T27RH55 (27°C, 55% RH, AH=14.2 g/m3, skin surface temp 29°C). Reference/control condition. Log P is permeability coefficient (log scale).

notes: Table I (page 3) provides vehicle composition (wt%) shared across all formulations. Table III (page 6) provides skin permeation parameters (Log P, DL−2, KL) for IP (ibuprofen) and ANP (antipyrine) under 4 environmental conditions. Only ibuprofen (IP) rows are extracted here. The ENDPOINT_TABLE_HINT is Table 3 (=Table III in the paper). The endpoint_kind field is set to 'percent' as a proxy since 'Log P' (log permeability coefficient) does not map exactly to the available enum options; the actual endpoint is Log P (permeability coefficient, log-scale, cm/s implied). DL−2 and KL values are noted in evidence_snippet and notes but are not separately emitted as endpoint records since the hint prioritises the primary permeation parameter. The table is truncated (pages 5 and beyond only partially available), so Table 3 data for AMP, ANP, MP are not fully visible for ibuprofen-only scope. Receptor fluid maintained at 32°C; Franz diffusion cell; effective diffusion area 1.77 cm2; sampling hourly for 8 h after 1-h acclimation.

Quantitative transdermal endpoint data (cumulative permeation µg/cm² vs time over 24 h, and steady-state flux µg/cm²/h) for ME/Gel, Gel, and Cream are presented only in Figure 3 (graphical), not in a separately tabulated form within the provided page text. components.remark: ~3x solubility added to purified water, stirred 37°C 24h

formulations.evidence_snippet: ibuprofen permeation rates from the suspended aqueous solution were 7.92 ± 0.39 and 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h, respectively. mean ± S.D., n = 3
formulations.notes: dQ/dtsol(Av=1) for ibuprofen from suspended aqueous solution = 2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h (n=3). Steady-state flux calculated from slope of cumulative permeation from 1 to 8 h.

notes: Table 2 provides the complete molar fraction composition of all 11 IL preparations (#0–#10) using lidocaine (cation) and ibuprofen (anion). Each row has been extracted as a separate record. No numeric endpoint table was found in the extracted text pages; endpoint data are reported as figures (Figs. 3, 4) and a single in-text steady-state flux value for ibuprofen from suspended aqueous solution (2.35 ± 0.20 µmol/cm²/h, Av=1, n=3). Steady-state flux (fluxss) was calculated from slope of cumulative permeation 1–8 h through 75 µm silicone membrane; receptor = PBS pH 7.4, temperature = 37°C, donor volume = 3.0 mL. The truncation note indicates PDF page text cap was reached; a numeric endpoint/flux summary table (likely Table 3 or similar) may exist on pages not captured. Manual review recommended to retrieve tabulated fluxss values per preparation.

title: A new vesicle-loaded hydrogel system suitable for topical applications: preparation and characterization.

formulations.evidence_snippet: Table 1. Vesicle dimensions (diameter), polydispersity index (p.i.), ζ-potential values and vesicle EE (%). Empty-NSV 138±1 0.051±0.03 -39±1 ----
formulations.notes: Empty NSV; Size 138±1 nm, p.i. 0.051±0.03, zeta-potential -39±1 mV, EE not applicable

formulations.api_basis: added to surfactant/cholesterol mixture before vesicle preparation
formulations.notes: NSV loaded with 1% IBU; Size 150±2 nm, p.i. 0.130±0.01, zeta-potential -44±2 mV, EE 0.4±0.1%

formulations.api_basis: added to surfactant/cholesterol mixture before vesicle preparation

components.remark: from Xanthomonas campestris; Mw 1.25×10^6 g/mol; LBG:xanth weight ratio 1:1

formulations.evidence_snippet: even after 48 h, the release was not complete and only about 60% of the loaded drug was capable to diffuse from the matrix (Figure 3a)
formulations.notes: Release at 32°C; ~60% IBU released after 48 h from NSVs-gel system (Figure 3a). Release medium gently magnetically stirred. Gel prepared at 60°C. EE of IBU in NSVs: 0.4±0.1%; release studies carried out on non-purified samples.

notes: The paper describes niosome (NSV)-loaded LBG/Xanthan hydrogel systems with ibuprofen (IBU) as a model drug. Table 1 provides vesicle characterization data (size, p.i., zeta-potential, EE) for all formulations including NSV-IBU 1%. Release data (~60% IBU released after 48 h from NSVs-gel system) are reported in the text and Figure 3a, not in a dedicated endpoint table. No explicit permeation membrane setup is described; this is a release (diffusion from gel matrix) experiment. The receptor medium is HEPES buffer (200 cm³) at 32°C. Endpoint values for earlier timepoints are not explicitly tabulated. TABLE_TRUNCATED is True; additional tables (e.g., Figure 3 data) may reside on later pages not provided.
